Welcome to Butts and Seats and Bucks in the Bank. If you're serious about succeeding in the seminar business, then buckle your seatbelts and get ready. Because Dan Kennedy and Bill Glazer, two of the most successful and profitable seminar marketers on the planet, are finally spilling the beans on how to put Butts and Seats and Bucks in the Bank, including their closely guarded secrets for filling large rooms and creating big paydays with seminars in any economy. With nearly 50 years of collective experience and over $100 million generated from seminars attended by over one million people, including their own perpetually sold out and highly anticipated annual seminars, Dan Kennedy and Bill Glazer are the envy of the seminar marketing world. And here's a golden opportunity to peek behind the curtains to see exactly how they're able to consistently extract maximum profits from their events. Dan and Bill share their profitable insights monthly in the famous No BS marketing letter, the largest and most read publication of its kind. Dan also shares specific information on marketing to the affluent, those least and last affected by the roller coaster economy and least likely to make buying decisions based on price in his monthly marketing to the affluent newsletter. If you're not already receiving these monthly newsletters, you're invited to take a free test drive of Glazer Kennedy's elite gold membership to claim $633.91 worth of pure money making information, including two issues of the No BS marketing letter, visit dankenny.com. You'll also have the opportunity to upgrade your free trial membership to receive the marketing to the affluent letter. Number one, actually, we'll do in this as the sort of David Letterman format, we'll be counting backwards. Number seven is mindset of the list. So what is the mindset of the list? And the last couple of years, given the down economy, the truth of the matter is, in many cases, it's been harder to get people to come to seminars. Most of the people that I personally consult with and are running seminars, they're finding it more difficult to fill their seminars because the down economy is very much on people's minds. So they're saying, gee, maybe I shouldn't be spending the money to come to seminars, the truth of the matter is, it's probably more important in the down economy to actually find ways to improve your business or also ways that you didn't even know about that you could be improving your business or finding the business within the business. But that doesn't change the fact that people do have a mindset of why they should not be coming to seminars from different times of the years. So one of the number seven factors, the mindset of the list of your prospects that you are actually communicating with. Number six is marketing funnel. Is the marketing funnel. Most people underestimate the difficulty of the job and their marketing funnel is way too small in order to get people to come to events at Glaser Kenny. Just this event alone, over 90 different marketing touches. Many of them are online, that they're Warners costly. Some of them are offline, some of the offline stuff isn't as costly. Some of it is very, very costly, but over 90 touches. So most people don't begin to do enough marketing to get people to come to those events. I know that in the room, there's many, many great independent business advisors, but we do that battle every day with our independent business advisors to get people to come to their local chapters. They don't begin to do enough marketing. There's not enough of a marketing funnel. And with that, there's two things that affect that dramatically, is diversity of the media. So not just using one media. If you're just using online, you're crazy. If you're just using email marketing online, you're crazy. So it's a diversity of the media. And the second thing is lead time. Is are you giving yourself sufficient amount of time to do the marketing? We typically at Glaser Kenny, when we go into full force in marketing event, we give ourselves six months lead time in order to do the job the right way. Six months, we think in terms of. And those of you that I'm looking at around the room that I have done some consulting with, you've heard me talk about this over and over again, it's not doing enough of it. Also looking around the room, I don't wanna point anybody out, so I'm trying not to make eye contact. Those of you in the room that I was looking around who give them, do enough diversity, do enough, because I know them, do enough diversity, give themselves enough lead time, and do enough marketing, they have very successful events and they've learned that. So there's that point of fact, the marketing funnel itself, and the two key factors are diversity, not putting all your eggs in one basket. Number two is your lead time to fill it. Number five is size of the list. Obviously the more people that you got, that you can invite, the better chance you got. It's a numbers game. So the size of the list, so everything sort of always moves back down to at the end of the day to build the list. We're all in the list building business. We should all be in the list building, but it's for many reasons that we should always be building our list constantly. And including the fact that you always will lose prospects and customers, so you have to constantly replace them as well. So there's list building, which is the reason why tomorrow we are doing the herd building day to teach you ways to build a list, and also to care for the list. Number four is knowledge about the list. And this is really important because this allows you to do segmentation. The best result that you can get in any marketing that you do is the result that the person who's receiving your message says to themselves, hey, this person is speaking to me. This person is speaking directly to me. So there is seminar attendees versus non-entendees. That's one way to think about segment your list. Number two is customers versus prospects, whether they've bought from you before, never bought from you before. Location of people on the list. So are they close to where the event is or are they coming from far distances? Contact information that we have about them. Do we have their email address, their email address? Do we have, can we text them, can we fax them? Can we call them by phone, pick up the phone and call them? The more of that we have, the more media that we can actually use. Previous purchase history. So what do we know about them? Have they bought a product in the past? So like for example, if somebody were to go to our resource center and buy the A to Z Blueprint seminar, they are raising their hand and they are saying the fact that I'm interested in information marketing. So those people should be getting a separate message, which is in our case they do, a separate message telling them that, hey, we noticed that you invested this. Obviously you're interested in information marketing. If you want to be at the premier event in the country where the best information marketers come to once a year, then this is for you. So knowing about their purchase history makes it infinitely easier to get people to say, gee, this person is speaking to me. Whether they are a previous seminar attendees and that is different than the first bullet. And so therefore, once again, is making a relevant topic or theme that is different than before, giving it a reason for existence is so important. Number two is being influential. That's number two, is being influential. If those of you who do not have Dan's influential writing workshop, you are just short changing yourselves. It's actually influential writing and communication workshop, teaching you how to be a person of influence. The job of the sale and any sale that you have becomes infinitely easier if you are someone who is influential, where somebody wants to be with you because of the fact that you are someone who is influential. So being influential is important. Everything that we do at Glazer Kennedy is all built on that platform of being people of influence. Number one is relationship with a list. Does a wooden list know you? Do they trust you? If they know and trust you, that is the number one thing you have to have. And the reason why I say that is because I'm always seeing how people try to go into the seminar business without doing all this stuff. What they do is they figure, this is, you know, they come into a room like this here and they see 800 people here, and they do the math, so somebody average paid $2,000 to be here, so 2,000 times $800, and you say, gee, this is an easy business. I'm gonna just do this business, okay? They don't realize that what it takes to do that. So therefore you have to really earn the right. And I've written articles about it, to earn the right for being able to sell things to people. And that's really what this is all about, is earning the right and having the relationship with the list and being influential are the two key factors that gives you earning the right in order to ask people to do business with you. So with that said, let's get started to repurpose with power. What do I mean by repurpose with power? What do we do at Glaser Kennedy, and what do I suggest that you do? What we do at Glaser Kennedy is we start with our main piece of marketing. We start with our main piece of marketing, and we always build that. Quite frankly, that is the biggest job that we do in our marketing is we first start with our main piece of marketing. How do we do that? We build a multi-page sales letter that we put everything in there, everything in this multi-page sales letter. And from that multi-page sales letter, we repurpose everything out of that. So then how do we repurpose that? Then after that, success stories, there. Now this is the website repurposed. So we're rolling out the red carpet, and this went online, it scrolled on down all the way to the end of the sales letter that I just showed you offline. We repurposed it for our online sales letter. Today, we do a lot of video sales letters, as all of you probably have seen. Our video sales letters are repurposed. I take the same content that I do in my big main sales letter, my big main sales letter, and I actually turned that into a video sales letter where the text comes up on screen as somebody is actually reading it. So it's the exact same thing. How do I do that? What I do is I take my main sales letter, the main print of the sales letter, and I get out two really valuable instruments that are hard to find, but you can find if you search enough for them, it's a ruler and a pen. Those two things, and I put lines under each section that I wanna cut out to make a message that I use. So like for example, that is my online sales letter, then I start moving to emails. And I'll take the sales letter and I'll chop it into, in this particular case, 18 emails. I took my main sales letter and I chopped it into 18 emails that I distribute over usually six weeks. Over six weeks, we send out 18 emails, each one with a piece of, that came right out of the main sales letter. So that's how I repurpose it. So what's important about that, why I tell you that, is because everybody thinks the job of writing the copy, they make it harder than it needs to be. The job of writing the copy is to create your main big sales letter, main, main big sales letter. Then after you do that, then you repurpose that and use it as many ways as you possibly can. Everybody get that? Got it? Yes? Right. So it really isn't as difficult as you think it is. It is difficult to write that main sales letter. But once you got that, the rest of it is now just repurposing it and moving forward with it. So there's a couple emails that are chopped out of there. Okay, the second thing is celebrity. This is a special message that goes to people who live outside of the United States. This is list segmentation, and it makes a big, big difference in how people respond. This one is an interesting one in the list segmentation department, which is actually an endorsed mailing. So like for example, this is a mailing that Infusion mailed out to all their customers saying to them, because you are a customer of Infusion, I'd like to invite you to come to the seminar that I'm speaking at, which in this particular case, Clay Massel speaking at, and also at that seminar, we did a special training for Infusion users. That's a specialized segmented mailing that we mailed out to other folks. So again, the question that you need to be thinking to yourself is what information do I know about my list? How can I slice and dice this information in order to make it relevant to the thing that I am actually offering folks? Again, I just want to tell you something. If I was up here right now, not talking about information marketing, not talking about seminar marketing, and if I was talking to a group of restaurant owners, if I was talking to a group of mortgage brokers, if I was talking to a group of dentists, God forbid I was talking to a group of lawyers, all those things together, I would be teaching them the same thing. The way you win the battle is by matching your message to your specific market so that people say to themselves, this person is speaking to me. Okay, next is diverse media. So, and again, I have a lot of, in my mastermind groups, I have a lot of these guys that are pure internet marketers. Thank God not all of them, but a lot of them. They do outnumber the real estate investing guys, so especially in this economy. So, but I have a lot of them in there. And I had this battle with them all the time. They wanna completely win the battle of filling their seminars, but just sending out emails. And I'm telling you that the ones that listen to me, they get this, that they don't just send out emails. I mean, so like, I'm looking over there, Lloyd Irving's sitting over there, okay? So Lloyd doesn't have this problem. Lloyd is very well recognized for lots of things, but one of the things he's well recognized for is the fact that he's a very, very bright internet marketer. But when you listen to everything that he does, he just did a seminar for people in the martial arts business. He used all kinds of media, not just online stuff. So he doesn't fall prey to that. The ones that use diversity in their media, they always will win this battle over the people that just use one kind of media. And I don't get it. I'm picking on email marketing. It could just be direct mail. You don't wanna just use one thing. So let me show you a couple examples. So first of all, here's newsletter's inserts. By the way, this should be familiar to you. This is a repurposed product. We took the main sales letter and pulled out of their segments that we put as newsletter inserts in our NoBS marketing letter and also some of our other newsletters each month. It's a repurposed product, but we put inserts in our newsletters. That's diversity. Here's another one, our online TV show, our online media. We promote our events on our online TV show. Here's another one, which is our NoBS Not So Daily Easeen. It's another place that we promote our events. Here's another one. This is faxing. So this is actually a fax that we send, the two-page fax that we send out. So, and we use fax very sparingly, because it is the one media that more people get upset about than any other media that we do. So we don't overuse it. We just use it sparingly, but when we use it, it's still very effective. By the way, there's a lesson here about the two-page fax that I wanted to stop and tell you about, which is the fact that page number one is the message, and page number two is the order form. And it's really important, whenever you do faxing, is you always do a second page, which includes the order form. And the reason why is because when you do this, they don't have the order form in front of them if you don't fax them the order form. So they have to now go and find the order form, and or they have to go online to register. You are making it difficult for them. They are at the fax machine, fax them two pages, fax them an order form, so while they're at that fax machine, they can fill it out and fax it back. Let them use the media that brought them to the party to actually respond to it. So that's two-page fax machine, fax. Whenever you're doing fax, always use two pages and always send out the order form with it. Here's a voice broadcast. Now this is the text, but this is a voice broadcast. So we use voice broadcast, another type of media. We use it more often than we use faxing, but we don't overuse it. What I like using in voice broadcasts, we broadcast is I like using celebrity impersonators. So like I'm not sure which one this is. Oh, this is Superman. So this was, so we actually, we did a whole script on the whole Superman thing, look up in the sky, it's a bird, it's a plane, that kind of thing. So we like to do things that are kind of cute with it because people enjoy getting on their voice machine, you know, their answer machines, they enjoy getting more personality and so they don't get upset about it, they sort of say they laugh about it. So again, use outrageous advertising, which I teach in all your voice broadcasts. Newspaper advertising. So this is one that actually, I don't know if Dave, Dave are you in the room? There he is. This is one that Dave's in my Info Mastermind group. This is one that he actually uses for Canada and which he puts a post-it note on the outside of the newspaper that he does all the marketing for this event. And so he does a post-it note in the outside of the newspaper talking about the event and then actually inside he also has an ad in there that's also talking about the event. So what he usually does, doesn't have to, they both stand on their own, but he usually has a little post-it note driving them to the big ad inside the paper. Very, very effective for local events, is to use newspaper advertising still. And the good news is that it's become much, much less expensive to use it because those guys are traumatized right now by being a difficult media so you can negotiate real hard with those folks. Postcards. I like using postcards. This is kind of a cool postcard. This is one, I think this is a Roy Fatt postcard and on the other side of it he actually included the enrollment form on the other side of the postcard. So this is one side where he had his message and on the other side it's the actual form that you fill out and just send it right back. So it's right on the postcard. So postcards are a good type of media. This one is a Magalock. This is actually a Magalock. We usually do one of these every year. One year at our event we had, that was Allie Brown, that's actually up there. We superimposed her head. Matt Fury, myself and Dan, we created a Magalock. Magalock is a sort of four page, what looks like four page magazine ads. We create one which is called Insanity Fair, a playoff of Vanity Fair. So it's called Insanity Fair. And we send out this four page Magalock and on page two there's a sales letter. Page three there's the rest of the sales letter and page four there's the rest of the sales letter. So once again it's a Magalock. It's a different type of media. By the way, this is the whole thing that's right here. If you saw the original sales letter, the big multi-page sales that are usually around 20 pages, this is repurposed copy that I took out of the main sales letter and worked it into this. Again, I only wrote the main sales letter one time and in most cases I actually am having Dan Kennedy write the main sales letter only one time. So I just have to now take it and repurpose it. This is a DVD, another type of media to send out. So this is a DVD mailing that we would send out. So again, another type of media. Some people will go online, some people will read things, other people want to watch things. So this is a mailing where we sent out, which had a DVD inside. So inside that is a DVD. CD mailing. This is the best example of S&D you will have ever seen. I created the CD mailing for my BGS marketing system and Roy Fatt knocked it off exactly to the T. Every word that I wrote, he rewrote for his own business. But this is sending out a little booklet with a sales letter inside and a CD in there because some people will listen. I personally, if I had to pick one or the other, as far as which one I would use, I would use CDs over DVDs if I had to. But that's only if I had to. What I prefer to do is use them both. I want to use every type of media I can. Why do I pick CDs over DVDs? Because it's easier for people to consume CDs than to watch DVDs. Because you can put them in your car as you're driving around. In my car, I've always got a policy of these in there, so I'm using it sort of like it's a traveling university to always have me learn more. Demonstration. This is the one I talked about, the very seldom anybody ever talks about. This is a biggie if you want a real, real big takeaway. If you're looking for something that you want to do that very few other people are doing, demonstration is really, really important. Actually, Doug, where's Doug? Our cameraman. Are you in the room? Oh, get ready, because we're gonna do a live demonstration in a second. Okay, so one by demonstration is demonstrating the fact that this is the right thing to do. Demonstrating the fact this is the right thing to do. Infomercials, when you watch infomercials, they are a total demonstration. They're demonstrating the products that they are selling. It's a demonstration. And at any time they do demonstration of the use of the product, the efficiency of the product, they always get better sales. That same thing happens in seminar stuff. So like for example, here's a piece, somebody actually talked about the fact that they ripped me off on this. But I think this might be this year, I'm not sure. But here's a partial list of the smart Glazer-Kennie inner circle, no, it's an old piece, that I'd be getting together in Chicago for Super Conference 2007, April 20th to 22nd. And look at all the names that are up there. That's there for a reason, to show the fact that everybody else is coming. So if you're not coming, something must be wrong with you. That's a demonstration.
